era_context: The Late Bronze Age, mid-second millennium BCE. The New Kingdom of Egypt under the 18th Dynasty (Thutmosid period) was at its height; the Hittite Empire dominated Anatolia and the Levant; Mycenaean Greece was in the Late Helladic II period; the Shang Dynasty ruled China; and the Vedic period was underway in the Indian subcontinent. This was an era of palace-based empires, chariot warfare, and long-distance trade networks.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited archaeological record for this specific year)
The Americas (no written records; archaeological dating uncertain)

events.title: Eruption of Thera (Santorini) — Minoan Eruption
events.description: The catastrophic eruption of the Thera volcano (modern Santorini) in the Aegean Sea occurred during this period, though exact dating remains contested. The eruption was one of the largest in recorded history, with a Volcanic Explosivity Index (VEI) of 6–7. It devastated Minoan settlements on Crete and nearby islands, generated tsunamis, and ejected vast quantities of ash into the atmosphere, affecting climate and agriculture across the Eastern Mediterranean and beyond. The eruption is often linked to the decline of Minoan civilization, though the causal relationship is debated.

sources.name: Thera tephra layers and stratigraphic evidence from Akrotiri, Crete, and the Aegean
Radiocarbon dating and ice-core evidence (GISP2, Greenland)
Plato's account of Atlantis (Timaeus and Critias) — later source, possibly inspired by Thera

events.certainty_note: Radiocarbon dating and ice-core evidence place the eruption between ~1650–1550 BCE, with most recent consensus clustering around 1600 BCE. Assignment to 1560 BCE is conventional; the true date may be 40–100 years earlier. Stratigraphic evidence is strong, but absolute chronology for the Bronze Age Aegean remains contested.
cross_references: Related to decline of Minoan civilization (mid-15th century BCE)
Possible connection to Egyptian plague narratives (though dating is disputed)

events.title: Reign of Amenhotep II — Egyptian military campaigns in the Levant
events.description: Amenhotep II (r. ~1427–1400 BCE in conventional chronology, though some scholars place him earlier) was consolidating Egyptian control over the Levantine territories conquered by his predecessors. He conducted military campaigns to suppress rebellions and maintain tribute from Canaanite and Syrian vassal states. His reign is documented in temple inscriptions and administrative records, and he is known for his martial prowess and chariot expertise. The exact dating of his reign remains debated among Egyptologists.
key_figures: Thutmose III (predecessor, though Amenhotep II may have been co-regent)

sources.name: Temple inscriptions at Karnak and Luxor
Historiographic consensus on New Kingdom chronology

events.certainty_note: Egyptian chronology for the 18th Dynasty is debated. Conventional dating places Amenhotep II's reign c. 1427–1400 BCE, but some scholars argue for dates 50–100 years earlier or later. The assignment to 1560 BCE is speculative and may be significantly off.
cross_references: Part of the broader New Kingdom expansion under the Thutmosids

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Thera eruption is highly contested. While it is often placed c. 1600 BCE, some scholars argue for dates as late as 1500 BCE or as early as 1650 BCE. The causal link between the eruption and the decline of Minoan civilization is also disputed; some evidence suggests Minoan culture persisted and adapted after the eruption rather than being catastrophically destroyed. Egyptian chronology for this period is notoriously uncertain, with competing 'high,' 'middle,' and 'low' chronologies differing by up to 150 years. The assignment of specific events to 1560 BCE is therefore highly provisional.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1560 BCE is fragmentary and heavily dependent on Egyptian temple inscriptions, which are often propagandistic and selective. Archaeological evidence (stratigraphy, pottery, radiocarbon dating) provides material grounding but is subject to interpretation and dating uncertainty. The Thera eruption is well-attested archaeologically but its precise date is debated. Egyptian administrative records exist but are sparse for this specific year. There is a strong Western/Mediterranean bias in the surviving sources; records from China, India, and the Americas are either absent or undated. The 'high chronology' vs. 'low chronology' debate in Bronze Age studies means that events assigned to 1560 BCE may actually belong to 1610 BCE or 1510 BCE.

graph_edges.note: The Thera eruption contributed to the decline of Minoan civilization, though the relationship is debated and the eruption may have occurred earlier than 1560 BCE
Amenhotep II's military campaigns were part of the broader New Kingdom expansion; his reign may extend beyond 1560 BCE
